spec.description: Number of tasks currently in the scheduler queue waiting to be assigned to workers.
spec.title: Task queue length

spec.description:
Percentiles for how long tasks are sitting in a queue before they get assigned to a worker for execution.

This does not include the time it takes for a task to be placed in the queue after being created.
spec.title: Task queue time

spec.description:
Percentiles for how long tasks take to complete after being assigned to a worker. 

Tasks which fail or are cancelled are not included. 

This includes the time a task spends waiting for its downstream tasks to complete.
spec.title: Task execution time

spec.description: The total number of worker threads available. Each thread can execute one task at a time.

The "load" of a scheduler is calculated by the sum of the number of enqueued tasks and running tasks. 

It is represented as an instant (the load at the time of collecting metrics), along with several smoothed averages using exponentially weighted moving averages (EWMAs).
spec.title: Scheduler load

spec.description:
Per-second rate of task state changes, by state:

* **Created**: A task has been created but not yet enqueued. 
* **Pending**: A task is in the queue waiting to be run. 
* **Running**: A task is being executed by a worker thread. 
* **Cancelled**: A task has been canceled. 
* **Failed**: A task failed during execution.
spec.title: Task state changes

spec.description: Current number of schedulers running serving queries.

In-flight tasks across all schedulers, by state:

* **Created**: A task has been created but not yet enqueued. 
* **Pending**: A task is in the queue waiting to be run. 
* **Running**: A task is being executed by a worker thread. 
* **Cancelled**: A task has been canceled. 
* **Failed**: A task failed during execution.
spec.title: In-flight tasks

spec.description:
Per-second rate of stream state changes, by state:

* **Idle**: A stream has been created but either the sending or receiving side is not yet running. 
* **Open**: Both the stream sender and receiver are available and data can flow through. 
* **Closed**: The stream has been closed by the sender, and no more data will be sent.
spec.title: Stream state changes

spec.description:
In-flight streams across all schedulers, by state:

* **Idle**: A stream has been created but either the sending or receiving side is not yet running. 
* **Open**: Both the stream sender and receiver are available and data can flow through. 
* **Closed**: The stream has been closed by the sender, and no more data will be sent.
spec.title: In-flight streams

spec.description:
Percentiles for how long tasks are sitting in a queue before they get assigned to a worker for execution.

This does not include the time it takes for a task to be placed in the queue after being created.
spec.title: Task queue time

spec.description:
Percentiles for how long tasks take to complete after being assigned to a worker. 

Tasks which fail or are cancelled are not included. 

This includes the time a task spends waiting for its downstream tasks to complete.
spec.title: Task execution time

spec.description:
The "load" of a scheduler is calculated by the sum of the number of enqueued tasks and running tasks. 

It is represented as an instant (the load at the time of collecting metrics), along with several smoothed averages using exponentially weighted moving averages (EWMAs).

The value here shows the 1m EWMA load per scheduler.
spec.title: Load across schedulers (1m average)

spec.description:
The "saturation" of a scheduler is calculated by dividing the scheduler's load by the number of idle or busy threads. 

Saturation is used to determine how much can be done in parallel. 

Saturation values at 1 indicate that all load can be executed in parallel. 

Saturation values above 1 indicate that there is that amount times more load than can be done in parallel. 

Saturation values below 1 indicate that all load can be executed in parallel and there is additional capacity for more load.
spec.title: Cell saturation (1m average)

spec.description:
Current number of active tasks running across all schedulers. 

Active tasks are tasks that are currently running or in the queue waiting to be run. 

This value is also called the "load."

options.content:
# Thor scheduler

The **Thor scheduler** is the entrypoint into making requests into Loki's 
next-generation query engine. It is composed of two distributed components:

* `query-engine-scheduler` 
* `query-engine-worker`  

Currently, these compnents do not have access to querying data which has yet 
to be flushed to object storage. To work around this limitation, queries sent to `query-engine-scheduler` must 
only cover time ranges for which data is reasonably expected to have been 
flushed. 

More details about the components are in the other README panels and in the 
descriptions of panels below.

# query-engine-scheduler

The `query-engine-scheduler` component is responsible for converting 
queries into parallelizable units of work (called **tasks**) that are 
scheduled and executed on a set of `query-engine-worker` pods. 

Schedulers are primarily monitored through their task queue:

* **Scheduler load** describes the total number of tasks that are 
  actively running on a worker or in the queue. 

* **Scheduler saturation** describes the ratio of a scheduler's load 
  to the total number of tasks that can be scheduled concurrently 
  across all workers.  

The set of tasks for a query form a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG), where 
some tasks send results to other tasks for further processing, like 
aggregation. Tasks are roughly scheduled in depth-first traversal order. 

## Potential issues 

### No workers 

If no workers are connected to a scheduler, it will be unable to generate 
query responses, leading to query time outs. 

### Oversaturation and worker starvation 

Schedulers become oversaturated when they don't have access to enough 
workers for task execution. This will lead to queries finishing more 
slowly or timing out entirely. 

Worker starvation is a subcategory of oversaturation, where all workers 
are assigned tasks that depend on other task results. Such those other 
tasks cannot be scheduled, these queries will time out. 

Autoscaling is used to resolve both these cases.

# query-engine-worker

The `query-engine-worker` component runs tasks assigned by a 
`query-engine-scheduler`. Each worker has a maximum number of 
tasks it can run concurrently, called its set of **worker threads.** 

Each worker maintains a connection to all schedulers via a 
DNS lookup. Each worker thread picks a scheduler at random 
and waits for task assignment. After its assigned task has 
completed, it starts over, picking a new scheduler to 
assign itself to. 

## Task results 

Edges between tasks in the DAG are represented as **streams.** 
A worker forwards task results directly along a stream, either to
another worker (which is listening on the receiving end of the 
stream) or a scheduler (which is listening on the receiving end of 
the root stream).

spec.description:
Per-second rate of task state changes, by state:

* **Created**: A task has been created but not yet enqueued. 
* **Pending**: A task is in the queue waiting to be run. 
* **Running**: A task is being executed by a worker thread. 
* **Cancelled**: A task has been canceled. 
* **Failed**: A task failed during execution.
spec.title: Task state changes

options.content:
# WIP 

rfratto is currently working on this tab 
as a way to better understand individual 
workers.

spec.description:
Distribution of the time goroutines have spent in the scheduler in a runnable state before actually running. 

High values indicate that runnable goroutines are stuck waiting in line.
spec.title: Go scheduler latencies

spec.description:
Statistics for worker saturation, counting how many task slots are in use out of the total number of tasks a worker can be assigned concurrently.

Total is the total saturation across the fleet. 

Other values report aggregations across the fleet: average saturation, p99 saturation, etc.
spec.title: Worker saturation

spec.description: Average rate of the number of tasks completed per second across the worker fleet.
spec.title: Completed tasks per second

spec.description:
Average rate of the number of tasks completed
 per second per worker thread.

Generally correlates to task latency, but will increase if work is parallelized effectively.
spec.title: Completed tasks per second per thread

spec.description:
Per-second rate of stream state changes, by state:

* **Idle**: A stream has been created but either the sending or receiving side is not yet running. 
* **Open**: Both the stream sender and receiver are available and data can flow through. 
* **Closed**: The stream has been closed by the sender, and no more data will be sent.
spec.title: Stream state changes
